story:
Reagan Theriault stepped back from her easel, paintbrush tucked between her lips, weighing the blacks and greens she’d laid down like a quiet hymn to mold. She loved what others found repulsive. Her studio breathed damp earth and oil, held at seventy-eight percent humidity for “atmospheric authenticity”—terrible for electronics, perfect for her. In Boston’s art scene she’d carved out a stubborn niche; critics praised her technique and recoiled at her subjects. She’d framed one review that called her work technically brilliant but “more suitable for a textbook than an art gallery.” On the day of her new show, her gallery-owner cousin, Anna, bumped her into the back room to make space for a flashier painter. Reagan swallowed the slight and kept working. She was documenting beauty where no one wanted to look.

Across the river, Dr. Deirdre Lathurna peered into a microscope at Massachusetts General. At forty-six, she was an exacting environmental mycologist chasing fungi that could eat microplastics. Some strains—Aspergillus among them—nibbled at polyethylene but left toxic crumbs. On her way to Anna’s opening—out of family duty, not artistic interest—Deirdre expected nothing more than polite small talk.

In the gallery’s back room, she stopped dead. Huge, precise paintings of molds. Penicillium, Cladosporium, Alternaria—forms she knew at a glance. Then a canvas with a branching pattern she’d never seen: a naked-eye bloom rendered with uncanny consistency, its surface branching hinting at a deeper microscopic order—lace-fine tendrils, bulbous nodes at intersections, a teal iridescence that Reagan had inferred and stylized rather than literally seen. “Where did you observe this?” she asked the paint-smudged artist standing nearby.

“My bathroom ceiling,” Reagan said.

Deirdre introduced herself, astonished by the scientific accuracy on the walls. She asked to visit Reagan’s studio—and the ceiling.

By morning, Deirdre was in Reagan’s apartment, taking careful samples from the delicate network spreading across the plaster. In the studio, she sifted through years of paintings. The later canvases captured structures with unnerving precision.

“I think you’ve been documenting a previously undescribed species,” Deirdre said. “One that matches a theoretical model for breaking down polymers without toxic byproducts.”

She proposed a collaboration. Reagan, who “barely passed biology,” agreed anyway.

Weeks later, side by side at a microscope, Reagan watched her painted forms come alive on a slide. Early tests showed the mold produced an enzyme that degraded PET—common microplastics—cleanly. But the strain collapsed under standard lab conditions.

Reagan offered a guess. “My studio’s humidity and light.”

They recreated “studio conditions”—75–80% humidity, 68 degrees Fahrenheit, low targeted light—and the colonies stabilized.

Months on, they stood before a packed room at Mass General.

“As you can see,” Deirdre said, “the structure of Mycotheria verrucosa—‘Theria’s Lace’—is exactly as Ms. Theriault documented.”

Field tests showed a 73% reduction of PET in controlled samples over two weeks, with no detectable toxins. Reagan explained how “bathroom-ceiling conditions” made cultivation possible. Scientists crowded her afterward, eager to study other anomalies she’d painted.

Funding followed: a three-year Environmental Protection Foundation grant naming Reagan a co-investigator for visual documentation and cultivation design, plus a traveling exhibition. Their first paper arrived with both names on the masthead. Reagan cried.

A year later, the Boston Museum of Science opened “Theria’s Lace: Where Art Meets Microbiology.” Visitors watched live colonies eat plastic, then compared microscope photographs with Reagan’s paintings—not exact copies, but striking correspondences between what she had artistically perceived at room scale and what magnification later confirmed.

Asked about validation, she said, “It’s about recognition—that beauty can live in overlooked places.”

Deirdre added, “The breakthrough came from seeing differently.”

At the exit, a final canvas shimmered with fine iridescence beside an electron micrograph and a small plaque:

Mycotheria verrucosa (Theria’s Lace)
Named for artist Reagan Theriault, whose paintings revealed a pattern researchers had overlooked and led microscopes back to it.
A testament to finding the extraordinary in the overlooked.
